summary: Jeff Atwood documents his Core 2 Duo system build, explaining his component choices and overclocking strategy. He details why he selected the ASUS P5B Deluxe motherboard, midrange DDR2-667 memory, the E6600 CPU with 4MB L2 cache, and an oversized Scythe Infinity heatsink. His core philosophy is buying upper-midrange components and overclocking into high-end territory for better value. He successfully pushed his $319 2.4GHz E6600 past the $999 X6800 extreme chip's performance, achieving 3.15GHz through careful FSB and voltage adjustments, resulting in a 50% performance improvement over his previous system.
key_points: Buy upper midrange hardware and overclock to high-end performance for maximum value
Extremely fast memory is overrated since the cache-to-main-memory penalty already dominates performance
L2 cache size matters more than clock speed in CPU selection
A quality CPU heatsink is essential for quiet computing and enables aggressive overclocking
Responsible overclocking requires incremental changes and extensive Prime95 stability testing
money_quotes: My general strategy with computer upgrades is to buy upper midrange and overclock myself into high-end territory for extra value.
Computer hardware is one of my few indulgences, but I do a freakishly obsessive amount of research before buying anything.

I'm a big believer in cache, so I'm not willing to drop down to the E6300 or E6400 which only have 2 megabytes of L2. This might be a little irrational if you actually compare the performance, but so be it. I loves me some L2 cache.
If you want a quiet PC, buy the best CPU heatsink you can afford.
It's such beautiful, magnificent, glorious overkill. It barely fit in my case. That just made me love it all the more.
Sounds traumatic, but if you want to make an omelette, you have to break some eggs. Don't be afraid to break stuff.
key_insight: Smart hardware purchasing means buying quality midrange components and leveraging overclocking knowledge to achieve high-end performance at a fraction of the cost.
